item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do believe the Evangelion reference they are talking about is the entire planet being dead, with the corpse of a monster resembling giant version of the dominant native species laying rest on the surface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: edit: ignore, can't read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna give it a no-vote for the strange inclusion of memetic hazards where they didn't really belong. It feels like they were tacked on as an afterthought. Article would have worked fine without them.

EDIT: No idea what past me was on about. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or perhaps Dash-Omega was a kind of Giant Naked Rei on a less prepared planet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I've encountered the word "slurry" more on this site than in all other media I've consumed, combined.

This is understated and almost poetic. A rare instance where I'm thinking "thank goodness there aren't any exploration logs." It's definitely going on my "to make fan art" list, though.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: This is the most well-deserved +100 on the site. I'm not joking.
All's right with the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept, great execution. I’d say this hits that sweet spot of not giving away too much information but not [REDACTING] the whole thing either.

(Also are the evangelion references everyone is talking about the corpse circles/pillars?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article's part of an in-development space opera canon, Ad Astra Per Aspera. The first FTL drives are invented via highly anomalous and redacted methods in the 2020s (though they take far longer than a few days to travel distances like this).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good and intersting but got confused when the year is 2035 and the fondation already managed to create interstelar travel that takes only like a few days to cross 12 light years distance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's less decay by microbes and more "the structure is just falling apart on its own." No life is involved in the formation of the slurry; the death of SCP-3841-Ω lead to its internal structure and flesh destabilizing and slowly liquifying on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great read. One nitpick: "Decaying organic matter is attached to the sides of the skeleton, holding some sections together, which progressively converts into a reddish-brown slurry of organic chemicals that falls from SCP-3841-Ω." kinda contradicts "No living .. microscopic life have been found" - for something to decay requires microbes to decompose it. Microbes from its own gut perhaps? But these would still count as life, just not native life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant. Straight-up some crazy-good skip writing, it's super intruiging, and jam-packed with wonderful little details that are just killing me in wanting to know more.

The whole paragraph starting with "Little knowledge exists on Luytenian civilization." adds a great deal to this. It hits so many "What the Fuck" buttons it should be a crime, and does a wonderul job of deepening the mystery/strengthening the uniqueness of the K-scenario. I'm heavily reminded of the comic "Memetic".

Absolutely stellar. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote as the Evangelion references are a bit too obvious as to border on being derivatve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fair minority of SCPs are set in the (relatively) near future and some have greatly advanced space travel by both the Foundation and civilians, so some of those things are probably assumed.

I'm assuming 9volt wrote this for the Ad Astra Per Aspera canon (currently hub- and tag-less), a big recent example of this, although it's not explicitly stated.

(Also +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Were the Evangelion references intentional or am I just reading in to things?

In any case, I enjoyed this article. The imagery is vivid and the mystery behind the incident really gets the imagination going.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It'd be a Bionicle reference if there had been another SCP-3841-Ω that deliberately manipulated gravity to kill the first one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's no moon, it's an aborted XK!

Anyway… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have some issues with this article, specifically with regards to a consistent sense of the level of technology involved, and the physics.
The nearest exoplanets are around 12 light years away. In the containment procedures, it's mentioned that the planet's "atmospheric haze" would hide the surface, but at these kinds of distances, non-anomalous visual resolution of an exoplanet's surface from Earth is impossible. Not only would amateur astronomers not be able to see the surface, no one could. Which means that the Foundation must have initially sent probes to the planet, before the anomaly was found. It might benefit the article to include a description of the Foundation singling out the planet for exploration due to unexpected spectrographic readings, or something similar.
The line "Luyten b is not expected be habitable for the next hundred years, if ever." should be removed; it answers a question that isn't asked. Also, if the planet has a thick atmosphere with detectable nuclear fallout, it's going to be a lot longer than a hundred years.

Otherwise I do enjoy the basic conceit - seeing the now-settled aftermath of a planetary catastrophe, whose cause is as alien as the long dead inhabitants. However, and this is just my personal preference, this article would be a lot more interesting if it were overall more clinical in tone. It would better fit the premise, and the site as a whole, if it read more like a scientific planetary survey.
And to that point, you might consider changing "Luytenians" to a designation like "SCP-3841-1." It would be more congruent with your designating of "SCP-3841-Ω."

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. The image of sapient beings watching something approximating their own anatomy grow to such outrageous proportions as to draw and collide with the planet's moon, the event likely causing a mass extinction of the planet, is phenomenal. And the writing is out of this world good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

From the Luytenian perspective. I'd say this K-class event was thoroughly completed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn’t this how Makuta Teridax met his demise? Do I sense a Bionicle reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly the kind of thing I come to the site for.

I only wonder if you could have somehow left the exact cause of death a puzzle to be pieced together by the reader, but if you had done that I likely wouldn't have gotten it so shrug.

Stellar work. 2019 is off to a great start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's an article written in response to Bright's 1st Challenge. Note that I had the idea behind this article for a while (aborted alien K-Class event), but only today did it actually come together into something workable. This was written in roughly 4 hours.

Also, regarding the object class, I was leaning towards Neutralized, but there's still some anomalies (indestructible skeleton and the cognitohazards) present plus structures with anomalous origins, so I felt Safe would be more applicable in this case. This may be subject to change though.
